markdown.markdown:
::: {.landing-hero} Evaluation notes This section collects source-grounded study notes. The first collection covers Funnell and Rogers' *Purposeful Program Theory* [@funnell_rogers_2011_purposeful_program_theory].
Book notes Theory of change Logic models Export BibTeX
::: ```{=html}

``` ::: {.section-grid} ::: {.section-card data-note-card="" data-section="Part 1" data-type="Synthesis"}
BookSynthesis
### [Purposeful Program Theory](purposeful-programme-theory/index.qmd) Book overview, part structure, and reading routes for the chapter notes. ::: ::: {.section-card data-note-card="" data-section="Part 1" data-type="Synthesis"}
Synthesis
### [Core Concepts](purposeful-programme-theory/concepts.qmd) A consolidated guide to the book's central distinctions and recurring concepts. ::: ::: {.section-card data-note-card="" data-section="Part 1" data-type="Chapter"}
Part 1Chapter
### [1. The Essence of Program Theory](purposeful-programme-theory/chapter-01-the-essence-of-program-theory.qmd) Program theory turns evaluation from a simple before-and-after judgement into an explanation of how an intervention is expected to generate change, where that explanation might fail, and what evidence would distinguish success, partial success, implementation failure, and theory failure. ::: ::: {.section-card data-note-card="" data-section="Part 1" data-type="Chapter"}
Part 1Chapter
### [2. Variations of Program Theory over Time](purposeful-programme-theory/chapter-02-variations-of-program-theory-over-time.qmd) Program theory is not a new invention but a family of related ideas with varied terminology, intellectual roots, and practical traditions; evaluators need to understand the vocabulary well enough to choose terms that support use rather than confusion. ::: ::: {.section-card data-note-card="" data-section="Part 1" data-type="Chapter"}
Part 1Chapter
### [3. Common Myths and Traps](purposeful-programme-theory/chapter-03-common-myths-and-traps.qmd) The value of program theory depends on avoiding common myths and practical traps, especially the belief that drawing a diagram is enough or that one standard model can fit every evaluation purpose. ::: ::: {.section-card data-note-card="" data-section="Part 2" data-type="Chapter"}
Part 2Chapter
### [4. Scoping Intended Uses](purposeful-programme-theory/chapter-04-scoping-intended-uses.qmd) A program theory should be scoped around intended users, uses, and timing; the same intervention can need different theories for planning, communication, management, monitoring, evaluation, or evidence-informed policy. ::: ::: {.section-card data-note-card="" data-section="Part 2" data-type="Chapter"}
Part 2Chapter
### [5. The Nature of the Situation and the Intervention](purposeful-programme-theory/chapter-05-the-nature-of-the-situation-and-the-intervention.qmd) The nature of the intervention and its setting should shape the program theory; simple, complicated, and complex situations require different assumptions about focus, governance, consistency, necessity, sufficiency, and change trajectories. ::: ::: {.section-card data-note-card="" data-section="Part 3" data-type="Chapter"}
Part 3Chapter
### [6. Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory](purposeful-programme-theory/chapter-06-processes-to-identify-or-develop-a-program-theory.qmd) Developing a program theory is a methodological choice, not just a workshop activity; evaluators must decide who participates, what evidence informs the theory, how conflict is handled, how much resource to invest, and when to revisit the theory. ::: ::: {.section-card data-note-card="" data-section="Part 3" data-type="Chapter"}
Part 3Chapter
### [7. Developing a Theory of Change](purposeful-programme-theory/chapter-07-developing-a-theory-of-change.qmd) A theory of change explains the causal pathway from problem analysis to intended outcomes; it requires situation analysis, evidence-informed causal reasoning, careful focusing, and a defensible outcomes chain. ::: ::: {.section-card data-note-card="" data-section="Part 3" data-type="Chapter"}
Part 3Chapter
### [8. Developing a Theory of Action](purposeful-programme-theory/chapter-08-developing-a-theory-of-action.qmd) A theory of action explains what the program will do to activate the theory of change, including resources, activities, outputs, success criteria, assumptions, and factors that affect achievement. ::: ::: {.section-card data-note-card="" data-section="Part 3" data-type="Chapter"}
Part 3Chapter
### [9. Representing Program Theory](purposeful-programme-theory/chapter-09-representing-program-theory.qmd) Representing program theory is a design choice: diagrams, matrices, narratives, and multiple models should be selected to fit the intervention’s causal structure, complexity, and intended use. ::: ::: {.section-card data-note-card="" data-section="Part 3" data-type="Chapter"}
Part 3Chapter
### [10. Critiquing Program Theory](purposeful-programme-theory/chapter-10-critiquing-program-theory.qmd) A program theory should be critiqued for clarity, plausibility, evidence, context, ethics, usefulness, and proportionality before it is used as the basis for monitoring or evaluation. ::: ::: {.section-card data-note-card="" data-section="Part 4" data-type="Chapter"}
Part 4Chapter
### [11. Some Research-Based Theories of Change](purposeful-programme-theory/chapter-11-some-research-based-theories-of-change.qmd) Research-based theories of change can strengthen program theory when they are selected critically and adapted to the intervention context rather than imported mechanically. ::: ::: {.section-card data-note-card="" data-section="Part 4" data-type="Chapter"}
Part 4Chapter
### [12. Some Common Program Archetypes](purposeful-programme-theory/chapter-12-some-common-program-archetypes.qmd) Common program archetypes help evaluators recognize recurring intervention logics, but each archetype brings different assumptions, risks, and evidence needs. ::: ::: {.section-card data-note-card="" data-section="Part 4" data-type="Chapter"}
Part 4Chapter
### [13. Logic Models Resources](purposeful-programme-theory/chapter-13-logic-models-resources.qmd) Logic model resources and technologies can support representation, but the usefulness of a logic model depends more on conceptual clarity than on the drawing tool. ::: ::: {.section-card data-note-card="" data-section="Part 5" data-type="Chapter"}
Part 5Chapter
### [14. Developing a Monitoring and Evaluation Plan](purposeful-programme-theory/chapter-14-developing-a-monitoring-and-evaluation-plan.qmd) Program theory can anchor monitoring and evaluation plans by identifying what to measure, when to measure it, what comparisons are needed, and how evaluation questions connect to the expected causal pathway. ::: ::: {.section-card data-note-card="" data-section="Part 5" data-type="Chapter"}
Part 5Chapter
### [15. Causal Inference](purposeful-programme-theory/chapter-15-causal-inference.qmd) Causal inference in program theory should be both scientific and pragmatic, combining congruence analysis, counterfactual comparisons, and critical review to assess whether the program plausibly contributed to observed outcomes. ::: ::: {.section-card data-note-card="" data-section="Part 5" data-type="Chapter"}
Part 5Chapter
### [16. Synthesis and Reporting](purposeful-programme-theory/chapter-16-synthesis-and-reporting.qmd) Synthesis and reporting should use program theory to integrate diverse evidence, explain causal contribution, support timely learning, and connect findings across linked evaluations. ::: ::: ```{=html} ```
